If, once you have a specific date and location for care on the West Coast, you’d like us to check eligibility again, just let us know—we’ll be happy to restart the screening then.  

Should you have any general questions in the meantime (for example, about mobility requirements on our small volunteer aircraft, the kinds of trips we can help with, or how flights are donated), feel free to ask.

turns.question: q6
turns.assistant_response:
Here’s some general information that may help you plan ahead:

1. In-flight medical care  
   • Angel Flight West flights—whether on a volunteer pilot’s small plane or on an airline ticket we provide—do not include nurses, monitors, or medical equipment on board.  
   • Passengers need to be medically stable enough to fly like a typical airline passenger: able to sit upright with a seatbelt and not require hands-on care during the trip.  
   • If you think you might need oxygen or other equipment, talk with your doctor first; our coordinators can often work with you on arrangements once an appointment is set.

2. Mobility  
   • For volunteer-pilot flights, you must be able to step up into a small aircraft (similar to climbing into a tall pickup truck), move into the seat on your own, and bear your weight during boarding.  
   • For commercial flights, you need to be able to board and sit in a standard airline seat safely. Airlines can supply aisle chairs, but they still expect passengers to transfer themselves or with help from a travel companion—not flight crew.

3. Next steps  
   • When you have a confirmed appointment—date, time, and destination clinic—feel free to contact us again. We can then do a fresh eligibility check and, if it looks like a fit, help you submit a flight request.  
   • If mobility or medical stability varies day to day, it helps to discuss the expected flight date with your healthcare provider so you and we can be confident it will be a safe trip.

I hope this clarifies what to expect. Let me know if you have any other questions about how our program works.

turns.question: q7
turns.assistant_response:
No problem—here’s the general companion guidance so you can think ahead:

1. Volunteer-pilot flights (small private aircraft)  
   • Most planes have room for the passenger plus 1—sometimes 2—companions.  
   • Space and weight are limited; each seat counts, and everyone shares a small baggage allowance.  
   • Infant or child seats must fit in the aircraft, and pets or service animals must be able to ride safely.  
   • The volunteer pilot will confirm exact seating once a flight is matched.

2. Commercial airline tickets arranged by Angel Flight West  
   • We can provide up to four total tickets per patient per calendar year (patient plus companions).  
   • Airlines require that minors have an adult escort; otherwise, the choice of who travels is up to you.  
   • Standard airline baggage, pet, and service-animal rules apply.

If you’re unsure how many companions you’ll need, that’s okay. You don’t have to make a final decision until it’s time to submit a flight request—after you have a confirmed appointment. When that day comes, let your coordinator know the number of travelers you’d like, and they’ll see what’s possible within these limits.

Feel free to ask if you have any other questions about planning.

turns.question: q8
turns.assistant_response:
That’s completely fine. When the time comes to submit a flight request, we simply ask whether paying for the trip would create a hardship—no tax returns or bank statements are required. A brief statement like “travel costs would be difficult for me right now” is usually enough.

Because flights are donated, we just need to be sure the service is going to people who truly need the help; exact income figures are never required.

If you decide to move forward after you have a confirmed medical appointment, your coordinator will guide you through that quick question then. Until then, there’s no financial information you need to share.
